title: Whakapapa of the Flax Weaver: Uncovering Stories Through Raranga / Te Whakapapa o te Kaiwhatu Harakeke: Te Hurahura i ngā Kōrero mā te Raranga
subject: English
culturalContext: Students will explore the tradition of raranga (weaving) through the lens of local Ngāti Kahungunu master weaver Nanny Rina, who taught generations at the Mangakōtukutuku Stream where harakeke (flax) grows abundantly. The lesson connects to the college's namesake stream and the cultural practice of sustainable harvesting.
learningObjectives: Analyze and compare oral histories of local weavers to identify narrative structures and cultural perspectives
Create a written profile of a local weaving practitioner using descriptive language and culturally appropriate terminology
Compose and present a short personal narrative connecting to the tradition of raranga using appropriate oral language features
Apply understanding of sustainable harvesting practices through reflective writing about kaitiakitanga

activities.title: Whakarongo: Listening to the Weavers' Stories
activities.description: Students listen to recorded oral histories of local Ngāti Kahungunu weavers from the Mangakōtukutuku area, identifying key narrative elements, descriptive language, and cultural references in their storytelling traditions

activities.title: Tuhituhi: Crafting Weaver Profiles
activities.description: Using interview notes and research, students write detailed profiles of local weaving experts, incorporating Te Reo Māori terms for weaving techniques and materials while practicing descriptive writing techniques

activities.title: Kōrero: Our Flax Stories
activities.description: Students visit the Mangakōtukutuku Stream to observe harakeke, then create and present short oral narratives about their personal connections to weaving traditions or natural materials, focusing on vocal delivery and storytelling structure

activities.title: Whakaaro: Reflection on Kaitiakitanga
activities.description: Students write reflective pieces connecting the sustainable harvesting practices of traditional weaving to modern environmental guardianship, using specific examples from local practices and traditions

resources: Oral history recordings from local Ngāti Kahungunu weavers in the college archive
The book 'Ngā Mahi a te Whare Pora' by Diggeress Te Kanawa
Local weaving expert Hinewai Smith (community contact through college whānau network)
Mangakōtukutuku Stream access and harakeke identification guides from regional council

tasks: Written weaver profile demonstrating descriptive writing and cultural understanding
Oral presentation recording showing storytelling techniques
Reflective writing piece on kaitiakitanga connections

nzcAlignment: English: Listening, Reading, and Viewing - Processes and strategies
English: Speaking, Writing, and Presenting - Ideas; Language features; Structure
English: Achievement Objective Level 4 - Purposes and audiences; Ideas; Language features; Structure
The Arts: Understanding the Arts in Context - Level 4
